justification: The chapter argues that titles like 'Christ' were pre-existing political terms (section 'Opening lines 22-57') and that resurrection functions as political-theological legitimacy (discussed throughout Kingdom of God sections). The Testimonium Flavianum's 'appeared on the third day restored to life' and 'believed to be the Christ' are covered by the inventory's argument that 'Messiah is the authentic Judean royal title for an actual political ruler' (lines 445-464) with Josephus as cited evidence, and the general Greco-Christian framework argument.

The chapter argues that Kingdom of God was a political-institutional claim (lines 691-728) and that Jesus's message was political (lines 807-822). However, Miller's specific framing of Jesus within Josephus's narrative of insurrectionists and sign prophets — the specific argument that Josephus would have viewed Jesus as seditious alongside other troublemakers — is not presented in the chapter. The evidence (Josephus's broader insurrectionist narrative framing) is new.

The chapter does not discuss the triumphal entry on donkeys or Greco-Roman parallels of divine figures (Dionysus) riding donkeys. Neither the donkey-riding mythological trope nor Matthew's potential use of it for Gentile audiences appears in the inventory.

Same as IbLJSM4hDrw_0: Jesus enthroned on donkeys as divine Greco-Roman imagery is absent from the chapter inventory. Bernard Babtain's specific argument about Matthew's divinity portrayal through this imagery is not present.

The chapter already covers Alexander's divine birth and star tropes (section 'Jesus' birth was represented as a new star', lines 620-633) with evidence including 'Alexander the Great: new star trope' and Suetonius parallels. The Magi at Alexander's birth is a variant of the same parallel already presented in the star/birth section.

The chapter argues 'Son of God' was a standard Hellenistic title (lines 465-474) with evidence from Egyptian pharaohs, Alexander, and Ptolemies. However, the specific Genesis 6 Nephilim / 'sons of God' parallel to Greek demigods (Hercules, Achilles) as a shared conceptual framework is not presented. The argument exists; this specific comparative evidence is new.

The chapter covers 'Son of God' as Egyptian pharaonic title (Son of Ra, lines 465-474) and Alexander claiming Zeus-Ammon (lines 574-580). However, Canaanite royal ideology where the king was 'son of El' is not specifically presented. The Canaanite parallel is new evidence for the existing argument.

The chapter does not discuss Samson-Hercules parallels or Philistine-Greek cultural syncretism as a precedent for Greco-Hebrew interaction. Neither the argument (Greek influence embedded in Jewish tradition via Philistines) nor the evidence (Samson as Hercules-type character) appears.

The chapter explicitly covers Justin Martyr's First Apology 21-22 in section 'Opening lines 47-50': 'Christians propound nothing different from what you believe regarding those whom you esteem sons of Zeus (Heracles, Asclepius, Dionysus, Perseus)'. The specific evidence (Justin comparing Jesus's death/resurrection/ascension to Hermes, Asclepius, Dionysus) is already present.

The chapter argues Jesus's titles map onto Hellenistic royal vocabulary (lines 422-444) and covers Augustus as divi filius (lines 22-57). However, the specific evidence of Roman imperial apotheosis validated by sworn eyewitness testimony at the funeral pyre — and its parallel to Paul's insistence on witnesses to the risen Christ — is not presented. This is new evidence for the existing framework.

The chapter covers coins in Jesus's lifetime showing 'theos sebastos Kaisar' (Augustus Caesar as god) in section 'Evidence from inscriptions and coins' (lines 610-619) and the 'Render unto Caesar' exchange with coin equating Caesar with God. Crossan's argument about 'Jesus is my god' as political competition with Caesar on coins is the same argument with the same evidence already present.

The chapter argues that Kingdom of God was a political-institutional claim for earthly sovereignty (lines 691-728) and that Jesus's call to 'repent and believe' was political realignment under Roman occupation (lines 807-822). Crossan's framing of fishermen joining Jesus's movement as subversive political act against Roman economic exploitation is a specific illustration but the core argument and framing are already covered.

The chapter argues Jesus as 'God manifested in the flesh' continues the Hellenistic Epiphanes title (lines 505-512) with evidence from Antiochus IV, Ptolemy V, and Augustus/Horace. However, the specific examples of Pythagoras incarnating Hyperborean Apollo and Mark Anthony as new Dionysus are not presented. These are new specific parallels for the existing Epiphanes/incarnation argument.

The chapter does not discuss healing miracles as Greco-Roman proof of divinity. Neither the argument (healing as standard divine credential in Mediterranean religion) nor the evidence (comparative healing traditions) appears in the inventory. Chapter 4 discusses the baptism as coronation but not healing miracles as divine proof.

The chapter does not discuss hero cult tomb traditions or Paul's potential understanding of burial in demigod/hero cult terms. The chapter covers empty tomb implicitly through star/birth narratives but not burial-as-hero-cult specifically. Ch2 discusses the empty tomb but from a different angle (standard objections, lines 1283-1322). This specific hero-cult framing is new.

The chapter's existing framework argues that 'Son of God' and Hellenistic titles involve divine status (lines 465-474, 505-512). However, the specific articulation that resurrection meant 'taken from earthly realm to divine realm with elevated metaphysical status' — as opposed to mere resuscitation — is not explicitly stated in the chapter. The argument is implied by the framework but this specific formulation and the explicit apotheosis-vs-resuscitation distinction is new evidence/framing.

The chapter does not systematize the different mechanisms of divine translation (through virtue, royal status, suffering, or direct ascension). While individual examples appear (Alexander, Augustus), the systematic categorization of pathways to deification is not present.

The chapter explicitly covers Justin Martyr (First Apology 21-22) and Theophilus of Antioch in section 'Opening lines 47-50' with the argument that 'Early Christian apologists explicitly argued that Jesus's divine sonship used the same categories as Greek divine sonship.' The evidence includes Justin, Theophilus, and the Via Latina catacomb.

The chapter covers Kyrios as standard imperial honorific (lines 553-562) and Phil 2:11 in Acts/Epistles section (lines 324-331). However, the specific concept of theonymy (divine naming as mechanism of apotheosis) and the example of Livia's deification with divine name change are not presented. The Kyrios argument exists; the theonymy mechanism and Livia parallel are new evidence.

The chapter uses Josephus extensively for political-historical evidence (Messiah section, lines 445-464) but does not discuss how Josephus rewrites Jewish figures (Moses, Enoch, Elijah) using standard Greek divine translation vocabulary. The argument that Hellenistic frameworks shaped Jewish self-understanding exists; Josephus's specific rewriting of these figures with Greek apotheosis verbs is new evidence.

The chapter covers empty tomb through the star/birth narratives and Hellenistic framework generally, and Ch2 discusses empty tomb objections. However, the specific argument that disappearing bodies/empty tombs were a standard Mediterranean-wide signaling mechanism for divine translation is not explicitly stated in Ch3's inventory. The Hellenistic framework argument exists; the specific empty-tomb-as-standard-trope evidence is new to Ch3.

The chapter covers Psalm 2:7 as royal investiture formula applied at resurrection via Acts 13:33 (lines 51-53), and covers Phil 2:11 and Acts 2:36 in the Acts/Epistles section (lines 324-331). The argument that Jesus becomes Son of God through resurrection (adoptionist Christology) is present in the investiture formula discussion.

The chapter explicitly covers 'Josephus proclaims Vespasian as ruler foretold in Jewish prophecy (War 3.8.9)' and 'Yohanan ben Zakai greeting Vespasian as king' in the Messiah section (lines 445-464). The finding's argument about anointing language applied to Vespasian is the same evidence already present.

The chapter does not discuss the Nero redivivus myth — the belief that dead Nero would return to lead an army — or imposters claiming to be Nero. This is a specific Greco-Roman 'resurrection' parallel with political dimensions not present in any chapter inventory.

The chapter does not mention the Decithians or any Samaritan sect that maintained their messianic founder 'didn't taste death.' This is a new parallel for post-mortem messianic veneration outside both Judaism and Greco-Roman tradition, and neither the argument nor evidence appears.

The chapter argues Jesus competed with Greco-Roman divine figures using the same titles (lines 22-57). However, the specific geographical evidence of Caesarea Philippi — surrounded by temples to Pan and Augustus — as the setting where Jesus asks 'who do men say that I am' is not presented. The argument exists; this specific geographical-contextual evidence is new.

The chapter does not discuss Orphic tablets or their salvific resurrection theology tied to Dionysus. While the chapter mentions Dionysus in context of Justin Martyr's comparisons, Orphic burial tablets as evidence of popular resurrection belief and salvific practice are absent from the inventory.

Per the Global Q&A 'Mystery cult vs imperial cult under foreign rule' decision, the mystery cult framing is rejected but the institutional data should be extracted. The chapter does not discuss shared ritual elements (baptism, food/drink consumption, salvation theology) across Hellenistic religious movements. The chapter discusses Eucharist-like parallels implicitly via the Lamb of God section but not systematically. The institutional parallels are new evidence for the existing imperial-cult-under-foreign-rule framework.

The chapter covers John's Logos theology (lines 486-504) and the Hellenistic philosophical framework. However, the specific Jacob's ladder passage (John 1:51) interpreted as Greek cosmic axis imagery replacing multiple divine mediators (Hermes, Iris) with a single mediator (Jesus) is not presented. The Johannine Hellenistic framework argument exists; this specific cosmic-axis evidence is new.

The chapter argues that Greek and Jewish institutional frameworks coexisted (throughout the chapter). However, Adler's specific archaeological evidence of Jewish ritual immersion pools directly adjacent to a pagan Temple of Augustus under Herod is not presented. This is a concrete new archaeological datum for the existing dual-framework argument.

The chapter explicitly covers 'Augustus as divi filius on coins and civic festivals' in the titles section (lines 22-57). The specific evidence of Augustus styling himself 'Son of God' after Caesar's deification, with Caesar's spirit ascending with Venus, is the same evidence already presented.

The chapter discusses the Lamb of God title with both Jewish and Egyptian (Amun) parallels (lines 513-534). However, the Tibullus elegies from the Roman imperial cult era containing 'the lamb will die for you' as pre-Christian Greco-Roman sacrificial precedent are not mentioned. The Lamb of God argument exists; the Tibullus evidence is new.

The chapter argues Jesus's titles derive from Hellenistic royal vocabulary (lines 22-57). However, Adela Collins's specific hermeneutic commentary on Mark identifying massive connections between the Roman imperial cult and Mark's gospel is not cited. The argument exists; this scholarly reference and its systematic analysis of Mark is new evidence.

The chapter covers 'Messiah is the authentic Judean royal title for an actual political ruler' (lines 445-464) and Psalm 2:7 investiture formula with Acts 13:33 (lines 51-53). Romans 1:4 is covered in finding JbMPXOf8q2k_6 context. The Davidic messiahship argument with Pauline evidence is present.

The chapter explicitly states 'Christos: existing Greek word meaning anointed' and lists Hasmonean anointed priest-kings in the Messiah section (lines 445-464). The argument that anointing is a formal institutional political act analogous to inauguration is present.

This finding about Platonic philosophical concepts in the Exodus narrative and the Bible's structural parallels to Plato's political models is more relevant to Chapter 2 (which covers Greek philosophical influence on Jewish thought, the Eastern Mediterranean intellectual world) or possibly Chapter 5 (Paul and Greek cosmology). It does not directly address the Son of God title or Kingdom of God themes central to Chapter 3.

The chapter explicitly covers Justin Martyr comparing Jesus to Heracles, Asclepius, Dionysus, Perseus (section 'Opening lines 47-50'). Clement of Alexandria is also mentioned in cross-chapter inventory (ch6, Clement's Exhortation to the Greeks, lines 478-485). The Justin evidence is directly in the ch3 inventory.

The chapter covers these parallels through the Justin Martyr passage (lines 47-50) which names Heracles, Asclepius, Dionysus as sons of Zeus, and through the broader argument that 'Son of God' was standard Hellenistic royal terminology (lines 465-474). The specific narrative parallels (Heracles's labors/death/Olympian status, Dionysus killed/raised) are the content of Justin's comparison already cited.

The chapter does not discuss Q-document parables or their Homeric parallels. While ch4 discusses the Dionysian Gospel and Homeric mimesis in Mark, this specific Q-source parable paralleling Odysseus's servant story is not present in any chapter inventory.

The chapter argues Jesus was framed in Greek political-religious idiom (lines 1-57). However, the specific identification of Odysseus (victorious return) and Hector (suffering martyr) as dual heroic models for Jesus, with resurrection as the distinguishing factor elevating Jesus above both, is not present. Ch2 mentions Mark's Greek tragedy architecture (lines 1249-1281) but not these specific models. This is new evidence for the existing Greco-Christian framework argument.

The chapter argues Jesus's titles and framework are Hellenistic (lines 22-57) and mentions Justin Martyr's comparisons. However, the detailed Romulus apotheosis narrative — divine pre-existence, missing body, post-mortem appearances to witnesses, divine commission before ascending — is not presented in the ch3 inventory. Ch4 discusses baptism as coronation with Romulus parallels at lines 1014-1098, but ch3 does not have the Romulus apotheosis detail. New evidence for existing argument.

The chapter discusses Lamb of God with Amun parallel (lines 513-534) and ch4 covers the Eucharist's Dionysian parallels (lines 678-705, 691-694). In ch3 itself, the Dionysus sacred meal parallel offering immortality and divine transformation is not present. Per global Q&A, mystery cult framing is rejected but institutional data is extracted. New evidence for the existing framework.

The chapter argues for a Greco-Christian framework and Eastern Mediterranean synthesis (global Q&A decision). The specific observation that ancients 'freely associated resurrected divinities with one another' is not stated in the ch3 inventory, though the general syncretism argument runs throughout. This is a succinct new formulation supporting the existing argument.

The chapter does not discuss the Babylonian Akitu festival or the Attis cult as precedents for death-and-restoration establishing political authority. The specific argument that the king's ceremonial stripping, shaming, and restoration echoed mythic victory — as a mechanism for political legitimacy — is not present. Neither the Near Eastern ritual evidence nor this specific political-legitimacy-through-ritual argument appears.

The chapter covers Greek philosophical theology (Logos section, lines 486-504; Heraclitus, Stoics, Philo). However, this specific Orphic fragment about divine self-generation and unity ('one self begotten lives, all things proceed from one') is not presented. The philosophical-theological framework argument exists; this specific pre-Christian Greek theological text is new evidence.

The chapter covers Egyptian solar-royal culture (lines 211-223) and the star/birth section (lines 620-633). However, Plutarch's specific account of Harpocrates born on the winter solstice to Isis is not mentioned. The Egyptian connection argument exists; this specific nativity-timing parallel is new evidence.

The chapter does not discuss the parable of the wicked tenants or its Homeric parallels. Ch4 covers the Dionysian Gospel (lines 691-694) and Homeric mimesis generally, but this specific parable-Odyssey parallel (beloved son killed for inheritance / Odysseus killing suitors) with its dynastic succession theme is not present in ch3.

The chapter argues Jesus's framework is Hellenistic and covers Justin Martyr's comparisons (lines 47-50). However, Miller's systematic identification of five specific memetic signals (missing body, prodigies, darkness, son of god declaration, people fleeing) shared between the Romulus narrative and Mark 15-16 is not present. The Hellenistic framework argument exists; Miller's specific five-motif analysis is new evidence.

The chapter mentions Heracles and Asclepius through Justin Martyr (lines 47-50) but does not specifically discuss corporeal immortalization — the claim that these figures were immortalized in body, not just soul (Heracles procreating in heaven). The bodily-resurrection-as-deification-not-resuscitation argument adds new specificity to the existing Hellenistic framework.

The chapter covers this in 'Was God the Father the God of Moses or the God of Plato?' (lines 97-126), arguing that the identification of the Father with YHWH was not fixed and that Paul/John describe the Father in Greek philosophical categories. The section on Philo and Logos theology (lines 486-504) also documents Hellenistic Jewish philosophical synthesis. Collins's point about monotheism emerging through contact with Greek philosophy is the same argument.

The chapter explicitly covers Kyrios as both YHWH in LXX and imperial honorific in the Lord section (lines 553-562), and the Acts/Epistles section (lines 324-331) covers Acts 2:36 ('God made Jesus both Kyrios and Christ') and Phil 2:11. The dual function of Kyrios is directly addressed.

The chapter argues 'Son of God' was a comprehensive Hellenistic title (lines 465-474). However, Glover's specific four-quality model of ancient divinity (power, immortality, status, honor) is not presented as a conceptual framework. The argument exists; this specific analytical model is new evidence.

The chapter mentions Via Latina catacomb (Heracles raising Alcestis beside Jesus raising Lazarus, lines 47-50) and Good Shepherd as Hermes Criophorus (lines 535-544). However, the specific catacomb imagery comparing Jesus to Hermes and Theseus is partially new — Hermes Criophorus is already there but Theseus is not. Partially new evidence for existing argument.

The chapter covers Justin Martyr's First Apology 21-22 (lines 47-50) which is precisely this argument: Justin admitting the parallels ('Christians propound nothing different from what you believe regarding sons of Zeus') and defending Christian uniqueness. Justin's strategy of admitting similarity while claiming priority is the same content.

The chapter does not discuss Acts 14 or the Philemon and Baucis story from Ovid's Metamorphoses. Luke's deliberate imitation and inversion of this Greek myth to make a theological counter-point is not present. Ch5 mentions Luke's Greek literary culture (lines 1090-1105) but not this specific parallel. The specific evidence and the 'theological inversion of Greek myths' argument are new.

The chapter's framework supports Hellenistic categories for Jesus. The specific point about Luke 24 being the first and only time Jesus is worshipped in Luke-Acts, occurring after ascension (marking appropriate time for worship because he now 'lives in heaven and is a god'), provides new specific textual evidence for the existing apotheosis-framework argument.

The chapter does not discuss the Dioscuri parallel for James and John. The argument that Gospel writers structured Jesus's inner circle using Hellenistic mythological divine templates — with the disciples' request to sit at Jesus's right and left in glory paralleling Dioscuri art — is not present in any chapter inventory.

The chapter covers Logos theology (lines 486-504) and Philo's 'firstborn son of God'. However, the specific scholarly consensus that early Christians believed Jesus was 'an eternal pre-existent being who lived in the heavens, descended to earth, became incarnate' — as stated by Carrier — is a broader systematic claim not explicitly articulated in the chapter. The Logos section implies pre-existence but the full descent-incarnation-reascension schema is new framing.
The chapter does not discuss Orion, water-walking, or water-crossing as resurrection/rebirth symbolism. Neither the astrotheological argument nor the specific Orion evidence appears.

The chapter covers Augustus's divine titles including 'divi filius' and 'theos sebastos Kaisar' on coins (sections lines 22-57 and 610-619). The finding about 'Sebastos' meaning 'Holy One' or 'one to be worshipped' is the same evidence.

The chapter covers Augustus's divine titles and political theology (lines 22-57, 610-619) but does not specifically discuss the Priene inscription's 'evangelion' (gospel/good tidings) written for Augustus's birth as the institutional precedent for Christian appropriation of 'gospel.' The argument about political-theological language exists; the specific 'evangelion' inscription evidence is new to ch3.

The chapter covers Jesus teaching Greek (Platonic) model of immortal soul (lines 127-144) with evidence including Lazarus/rich man parable. However, MacDonald's specific identification of the Lazarus parable as echoing Homer's Tantalus in the Odyssey, and Plato's myth of Er as the structure for heaven/hell theology, are not presented. The Platonic-soul argument exists; the specific Homeric Tantalus parallel and Plato's myth of Er as sources are new evidence.

Per global Q&A, mystery cult framing is rejected but institutional data is extracted. Ch4 covers the Eucharist's Dionysian parallels (lines 678-705). In ch3, the Lamb of God section (lines 513-534) discusses sacrificial imagery but not the Eucharist sacrament specifically. The 'ingesting the god' parallel to Eleusinian kykeon and Dionysian practice is new evidence for ch3's framework, though partially covered in ch4.

The chapter mentions Dionysus through Justin Martyr (lines 47-50) but does not detail the Zagreus tradition — torn apart by maenads/Titans, descent to Hades, rising again as second birth. The Dionysus comparison argument exists; the specific Zagreus myth details are new evidence.

Per global Q&A, extract institutional data, reject mystery cult label. The chapter does not discuss Eleusinian mystery initiation's promise of salvation from death. The chapter argues Christianity inherited Greek institutional structures; the specific mechanism of initiation-based salvation theology is new evidence for how these institutions operated.

The chapter argues for Hellenistic divine sonship framework (lines 465-474) and mentions Justin Martyr on Heracles (lines 47-50). However, the specific argument that the missing body motif was widely interpreted as evidence of divine status transformation/ascension in Greco-Roman tradition — with Romulus becoming Quirinus and Heracles vanishing on pyre — is not systematically presented in ch3. New evidence for existing argument.

The chapter covers 'Son of God' as Hellenistic title (lines 465-474) and 4Q246 (line 46). However, Price's discussion of the Jewish divine council with 70 'sons of God' assigned as deputy gods to nations (Deuteronomy 32) is not presented. The 'Son of God' argument exists; the Jewish divine council and 70-nations delegation evidence is new.

The chapter covers Philo's Logos theology (lines 486-504) and Gnostics' denial of YHWH as the Father (lines 119-126). However, the specific mechanism of former deities being transformed into angels in later Jewish theology (referencing Philo and gnosticism) is not presented. The syncretic theological development argument exists; this specific mechanism is new evidence.

The chapter argues Jesus's titles derive from Hellenistic political vocabulary (lines 22-57). However, the specific term 'parousia' as a Greek political term meaning 'the arrival/appearance of an emperor visiting a city' (with Augustus inscriptions as evidence) is not discussed. Ch5 covers Paul as imperial envoy but not the parousia term specifically. New evidence for existing argument.

The chapter covers the Kingdom of God as political sovereignty (lines 691-728). However, Tabor's specific point about resurrection producing 'incorruptible glorious bodies' enabling believers to 'rule the whole cosmos' — resurrection as a mechanism granting political-cosmic authority — is not stated. The political kingdom argument exists; the specific cosmic-authority-through-glorified-bodies evidence is new.

The chapter covers 'Son of God' as Hellenistic royal title (lines 465-474). The extension of this title to all believers as 'glorified sons of God even above angels' is not presented. The title argument exists; the democratization of the divine-son title to the community is new evidence.

The chapter explicitly covers 'Josephus proclaims Vespasian as ruler foretold in Jewish prophecy (War 3.8.9),' 'Yohanan ben Zakai greeting Vespasian as king,' and 'Suetonius and Tacitus confirm Vespasian identified with Jewish oracle' in the Messiah section (lines 445-464). The exact same evidence is present.

The chapter argues Jesus's framework is Hellenistic (throughout). However, Eisenman's specific claim that physical resurrection is 'all Hellenistic greco-roman ideology' absent from 'any Palestinian tradition' and not found in the Dead Sea Scrolls provides a more direct statement than the chapter currently makes. The argument exists implicitly; Eisenman's explicit Greco-Roman-vs-Palestinian framing is new evidence.

The chapter covers 4Q246 with 'Son of God' and 'Son of the Most High' (line 46). However, the specific 'righteousness ideology' framing — where perfect righteousness makes one a 'real son of God on earth' with 'multitudes' of such sons — is not presented. The 4Q246 evidence exists; this specific righteousness-based sonship mechanism is new.

The chapter covers this in 'Jewish Silence' (lines 87-96): 'The earliest extant Christian record is entirely Greek: Paul's letters, Didache, 1 Clement, Ignatius, and the Gospels. No securely dated Hebrew or Aramaic Christian texts exist.' The argument that gospels are Greek literature written outside Palestine is present.

The chapter covers the Lamb of God title with Amun parallel (lines 513-534). However, Singer's evidence of pre-Christian pagan vicarious sacrifice theology (virgin and infant sacrifice in Aztec, Mayan, Central American cultures) as a widespread ancient motif is not presented. The sacrificial theology argument exists; the cross-cultural pagan sacrifice evidence is new. Note: the non-Mediterranean parallels (Aztec, Mayan) are less directly relevant to the Greco-Christian thesis.

The chapter covers 4Q246 (line 46) and the Messiah section (lines 445-464). However, 4 Ezra and 2 Baruch presenting the messiah as a celestial, quasi-divine figure who descends from heaven to carry out war are not cited. The messianic-title argument exists; these specific Jewish apocalyptic sources are new evidence.

The chapter covers messianic claimants and coins (lines 445-464, 610-619). However, Bar Kokhba's specific numismatic evidence linking his name to the Shema Israel and divine name as quasi-divine messianic claim is not presented. The coins-as-political-theology argument exists; Bar Kokhba's specific coinage is new evidence.

Ch4 covers the Dionysian Gospel and John-Dionysus parallels (lines 691-694). Ch3 does not discuss the Cana miracle. However, the argument that Jesus operates within Hellenistic divine categories exists in ch3. The specific Cana/Dionysus water-to-wine parallel with six jars as escalation is new evidence for ch3's existing framework, though ch4 covers the broader John-Dionysus relationship.

The chapter argues for Hellenistic framework (throughout). The specific argument that the empty tomb with women's amazement at the disappeared body is a standard apotheosis motif in Greco-Roman literature — 'very very common in this literature' — is not explicitly stated in ch3's inventory. Ch2 discusses empty tomb objections (lines 1283-1322) from a different angle. New evidence for existing argument.

This finding about Matthew's specific literary strategy of employing Stoic philosophical frameworks (removing Mark's exorcisms, presenting anger in Stoic terms) is more relevant to Chapter 4 (Gospels as Historically Reliable Sources) or Chapter 5 (where Greek cosmology in Paul and Luke is discussed, lines 1124-1197). Chapter 3 focuses on titles and Kingdom of God, not individual Gospel compositional strategies.

The chapter covers Jesus teaching the Greek model of the immortal soul (lines 127-144). Ch5 discusses Paul's Greek cosmology (lines 1124-1197). However, the specific Stoic/Middle Platonist philosophical framework for Paul's pneumatic bodies — 'flesh and blood does not inherit the kingdom of God' — is not in ch3's inventory. The Greek philosophical anthropology argument exists in ch3; Paul's specific pneumatic-body theology and its Stoic roots are new evidence.

The chapter does not discuss the Satyricon or its parallels to gospel narratives. The specific parallels (Trimalchio's mock funerary meal with body-dividing, rooster crowing as death omen, women at empty tomb) as evidence that gospel authors drew on Greco-Roman literary conventions are not present in any chapter inventory.

The chapter argues Jesus was framed in Greek political-religious idiom (lines 1-57). However, MacDonald's specific claim that Mark portrays Jesus as 'better than the Olympian gods' and 'better than Greek heroes' — not just better than Pharisees — is a specific new framing. The Hellenistic positioning argument exists; the explicit 'better than Olympian gods' formulation is new.

The chapter argues for Hellenistic framework and political-theological dimensions of resurrection. The specific scholarly observation that new resurrection/apotheosis stories emerged alongside 'new ideas about the boundaries between the living and the dead' connected to 'sociopolitical structures' adds a structural dimension. The argument exists; this specific sociopolitical-structures framing is new.

The chapter covers this in 'Jesus is the Son of Father' (lines 476-485): 'Plutarch: Plato's conception involved dynamis and pneuma' and 'Luke 1:35 uses same vocabulary (power and spirit) for Jesus's conception.' The exact same evidence — identical Greek terminology in Plutarch and Luke — is present.

The chapter covers Plutarch's account of Plato's divine conception with dynamis/pneuma vocabulary in the 'Son of Father' section (lines 476-485). The argument that divine sonship through divine conception existed in Greek intellectual tradition before Christianity is implicit in this section.

The chapter covers the Plutarch/Luke parallel (lines 476-485). However, the specific philosophical argument from Plato's rules of theology — God doesn't lie, doesn't change, sex is incompatible with divinity because it expresses human deficiency — explaining WHY non-sexual divine conception was philosophically necessary is not presented. The divine conception argument exists; the Platonic theological reasoning underlying it is new evidence.

The chapter covers Seleucid coinage and anchor symbolism (lines 588-598) and coins as political theology (lines 610-619). However, the specific argument that Flavian messianic coins bore anchor-and-dolphin imagery derived from Seleucid precedents, and that early Christians adopted these exact symbols in the Catacombs of Saint Domitilla, is not presented. The coins-as-theology argument exists; the Flavian-Seleucid-Christian symbolic chain is new evidence.

The chapter covers 'Son of God' as pre-existing Hellenistic political title (lines 22-57, 465-474), Augustus as divi filius on coins (lines 610-619), and the argument that Christian movement framed Jesus in Greek political-religious idiom. The finding about Mark 'daring to invoke this same language for an executed Galilean' is the same thesis already presented.

The chapter discusses Psalm 2:7 at baptism (lines 51-53) and ch4 covers baptism as coronation with dove as Zeus symbol (lines 1100-1106). However, the specific dove/eagle contrast — dove (peace, subjugation) vs eagle (conquest, dominion) as competing models of divine kingship — is not present in ch3's inventory. The baptism-as-investiture argument exists; the dove-eagle symbolic contrast is new evidence.

The chapter covers Jesus teaching Greek model of immortal soul (lines 127-144) and Paul at Athens quoting Greek poets (lines 97-126). However, the specific argument that Paul's 'spiritual body' resurrection theology reflects Platonic Greek dualism, and that Paul's education in Tarsus (center of philosophy) shaped this, is not stated in ch3. Ch5 covers Greek cosmology in Paul (lines 1124-1197). For ch3, this is new evidence for the existing philosophical framework argument.

The chapter explicitly covers this argument with evidence: 'Augustus as divi filius on coins and civic festivals' (lines 22-57), detailed titulature section (lines 465-474), and the conclusion that 'Son of God was a broadly accepted political title for the rightful ruler' (lines 906-909).

This finding about the earliest stratum of John as a 'Dionysian Gospel' with Jesus as donor deity (wine, living water, eternal life) is specifically covered in Chapter 4's inventory: 'The earliest stratum of John reads as a Dionysian Gospel built on deliberate imitation of Euripides' Bacchae' (lines 691-694). This belongs in Chapter 4.

This finding about specific John-Bacchae trial parallels (Pentheus interrogating Dionysus vs Pilate interrogating Jesus, crown imagery, purple cloak) belongs in Chapter 4 which covers the Dionysian Gospel (lines 691-694) and John's composition. Chapter 3 focuses on titles and Kingdom theology, not Gospel literary composition.

The purple cloak and Dionysian hemation imagery relates to Gospel of John literary composition, which is Chapter 4 territory (Dionysian Gospel section, lines 691-694). Chapter 6 also discusses purple as imperial symbolism. For ch3's focus on titles and Kingdom, this is better placed in ch4 or ch6.

The chapter covers Soter (Savior, lines 546-552) and Kyrios (Lord, lines 553-562) as Hellenistic titles. However, the specific comparison of Asclepius being called savior, lord, compassionate, merciful — with hymns 'you can almost transpose into a christian church setting' — is not presented.
